entry.question: What does the Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia Catholic baptismal record from circa 1889 identify as the parents of Adrian Zuñiga Rojas, born 8 September 1889?
entry.rationale: The Catholic parish baptismal record is the primary source type for identifying parents in late-19th-century Bolivia. FamilySearch's 'Bolivia, registros parroquiales, 1566-2020' collection covers Arani parish for this period (a 1916 Arani baptism is already in the tree). The compound surname 'Zuñiga Rojas' follows Spanish naming convention: father's paternal surname = Zuñiga, mother's paternal surname = Rojas — providing strong search anchors. This is the direct-evidence gatekeeper question; its answer will identify both parents and enable downstream verification.

entry.rationale: FamilySearch collection 'Bolivia, Baptisms, 1560-1938' (id 1520550) has 1.87M indexed records and is the highest-yield starting point. A baptismal entry for Adrian Zuñiga Rojas born 8 September 1889 in Arani would directly name his parents (father and mother listed in every Catholic baptism). The compound surname 'Zuñiga Rojas' indicates the father's paternal surname is Zuñiga and the mother's is Rojas, providing precise search anchors.

FamilySearch 'Bolivia, Catholic Church Records, 1566-2020' (id 1922463) is the broader umbrella collection with 9.8M records covering all sacramental acts. If the baptism-only indexed search misses Adrian due to name transcription variants (e.g., Zúñiga vs. Zuñiga, Adrian vs. Adrián), this wider collection may catch it. Also surfaces confirmations, marriages, and burials that could corroborate parentage.

Full-text co-occurrence search for the surnames 'Zuñiga' and 'Rojas' together across Bolivia parish records, executed via the search-full-text skill. In Iberian/Latin-American naming, a child's compound surname encodes the father's and mother's paternal surnames. Searching both as co-occurring terms (unscoped to any single collection) will surface the child's baptism, the parents' own marriage, or a parent's burial entry — even when the indexed name transcription is inconsistent. This is the highest-yield tactic when indexed search stalls on name variants.

Dedicated search for the marriage of Adrian's parents in FamilySearch 'Bolivia, Marriages, 1630-1940' (id 1520564, 339K indexed records). A Zuñiga-Rojas couple married in Arani before September 1889 would be Adrian's parents. The marriage record (a) confirms the couple as a unit, (b) supplies the mother's full maiden name (which death certificates and censuses often omit), and (c) by its date relative to Adrian's birth provides direct evidence of paternity — able to confirm or refute a father named only by indirect evidence. Volume search also shows Arani marriage registers 1877-1891 are 100% indexed.

Volume search returned multiple San Bartolomé, Arani image groups (prefixes 005091539–005091542) with no explicit record-type/date labels and 92–100% indexability. These are very likely baptismal registers from the parish church (San Bartolomé). Browse-only image navigation via image_search targeting the 1889 period may reveal Adrian's baptism when the indexed collections return no result. San Bartolomé is the known parish for Arani (confirmed by Gumercinda's 1916 baptism in the existing tree source).

Ancestry 'Bolivia Select Baptisms, 1560-1938' (collection 9795) mirrors FamilySearch data but uses independent indexing — name variants transcribed differently may surface here when FS indexing misses them. Also serves as corroboration if the FamilySearch result is found. Free fallback plan: try after FamilySearch and full-text approaches.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Parentage of Adrian Zuñiga Rojas (born 8 September 1889, Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia)

**Conclusion:** The preponderance of evidence strongly indicates that the parents of Adrian Zuñiga Rojas, born 8 September 1889 in Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia, are **Pedro Zuñiga** (father) and **Polonia Rojas** (mother), both of Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia.

### Primary Evidence

The most direct evidence comes from Adrian Zuñiga Rojas himself. His Catholic parish marriage record, recorded 29 November 1913 at San Bartolomé de Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia, lists his birth year as 1889 and names his father as Pedro Suñiga (an S↔Z transcription variant of Zuñiga) and his mother as Polonia Rojas.¹ This record is a derivative source (accessed via FamilySearch indexed extraction; the original parish image is inaccessible via MCP transport at 1.0 MB), but the parentage information is primary in quality: Adrian stated his own parents at his marriage. The compound surname he carried — "Zuñiga Rojas" — structurally corroborates this identification: in Iberian naming convention, the paternal surname is inherited from the father (Zuñiga = father's paternal surname) and the maternal surname from the mother (Rojas = mother's paternal surname), independently confirming Pedro Zuñiga and Polonia Rojas as the parental couple.

### Corroborating Evidence

Two additional, independently derived records confirm the same parental couple in the same parish:

**Cecilia Zuñiga's 1910 marriage** (5 February 1910, San Bartolomé de Arani): Cecilia Zuñiga (born approximately 1890), one year younger than Adrian, married Bernardo Rojas at Arani. Her marriage record, another derivative FamilySearch extraction,² names her father as Pedro Zúniga and her mother as Polonia Rojas — the identical couple. Cecilia is an entirely independent informant from Adrian: different person, different marriage, three years earlier.

**Josefa Zuñiga's 1922 marriage** (11 January 1922, San Bartolomé de Arani): Josefa Zuñiga (born approximately 1901), twelve years younger than Adrian, married Gervacio Rojas at Arani. Her marriage record³ likewise names her father as Pedro Zuñiga and her mother as Polonia Rojas. Josefa is a third independent informant, nine years after Adrian's own marriage and twelve years after Cecilia's.

Three independent informants — each a different child of the same parents, each at a different marriage event, each recorded by a different priest on a different date spanning 1910 to 1922 — all consistently identify Pedro Zuñiga and Polonia Rojas as the parental couple at San Bartolomé de Arani.

### Conflict: Manuela Rojas (1901 Record)

One record conflicts with the above evidence. The marriage of Juana Zuñiga (born approximately 1883) to Pedro Torrico, recorded 13 August 1901 at San Bartolomé de Arani,⁴ names the bride's parents as Pedro Zuñiga and **Manuela Rojas** — not Polonia Rojas. This discrepancy has been analyzed and resolved.

The conflict is assessed as follows: the three records naming Polonia Rojas (1910, 1913, 1922) represent three independent evidence units from three different informants, collectively outweighing the single record naming Manuela Rojas (1901) by a three-to-one preponderance. The chronological pattern is significant: Juana (born approximately 1883) predates Adrian (born 1889), Cecilia (born approximately 1890), and Josefa (born approximately 1901) by six or more years. The most plausible explanation is that Pedro Zuñiga was first partnered with or married to Manuela Rojas, producing Juana (born approximately 1883), and that following the dissolution of that union (by death or otherwise), he married or partnered with Polonia Rojas, producing Adrian, Cecilia, and Josefa. Juana Zuñiga is therefore likely a half-sibling of Adrian rather than a full sibling. The conflict does not undermine the identification of Pedro Zuñiga and Polonia Rojas as Adrian's parents.

### Research Scope and Limitations

## Parentage of Adrian Zuñiga Rojas (born 8 September 1889, Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia)

**Conclusion:** The preponderance of evidence strongly indicates that the parents of Adrian Zuñiga Rojas, born 8 September 1889 in Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia, are **Pedro Zuñiga** (father) and **Polonia Rojas** (mother), both of Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia.

### Primary Evidence

The most direct evidence comes from Adrian Zuñiga Rojas himself. His Catholic parish marriage record, recorded 29 November 1913 at San Bartolomé de Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia, lists his birth year as 1889 and names his father as Pedro Suñiga (an S↔Z transcription variant of Zuñiga) and his mother as Polonia Rojas.¹ This record is a derivative source (accessed via FamilySearch indexed extraction; the original parish image is inaccessible via MCP transport at 1.0 MB), but the parentage information is primary in quality: Adrian stated his own parents at his marriage. The compound surname he carried — "Zuñiga Rojas" — structurally corroborates this identification: in Iberian naming convention, the paternal surname is inherited from the father (Zuñiga = father's paternal surname) and the maternal surname from the mother (Rojas = mother's paternal surname), independently confirming Pedro Zuñiga and Polonia Rojas as the parental couple.

### Corroborating Evidence

Two additional, independently derived records confirm the same parental couple in the same parish:

**Cecilia Zuñiga's 1910 marriage** (5 February 1910, San Bartolomé de Arani): Cecilia Zuñiga married Bernardo Rojas at Arani. Her marriage record, another derivative FamilySearch extraction,² names her father as Pedro Zúniga and her mother as Polonia Rojas — the identical couple. Cecilia is an entirely independent informant from Adrian: different person, different marriage, three years earlier.

**Josefa Zuñiga's 1922 marriage** (11 January 1922, San Bartolomé de Arani): Josefa Zuñiga married Gervacio Rojas at Arani. Her marriage record³ likewise names her father as Pedro Zuñiga and her mother as Polonia Rojas. Josefa is a third independent informant, nine years after Adrian's own marriage and twelve years after Cecilia's.

Three independent informants — each a different child of the same parents, each at a different marriage event, each recorded by a different priest on a different date spanning 1910 to 1922 — all consistently identify Pedro Zuñiga and Polonia Rojas as the parental couple at San Bartolomé de Arani.

### Conflict: Manuela Rojas (1901 Record)

One record conflicts with the above evidence. The marriage of Juana Zuñiga to Pedro Torrico, recorded 13 August 1901 at San Bartolomé de Arani,⁴ names the bride's parents as Pedro Zuñiga and **Manuela Rojas** — not Polonia Rojas. This discrepancy has been analyzed and resolved.

The conflict is assessed as follows: the three records naming Polonia Rojas (1910, 1913, 1922) represent three independent evidence units from three different informants, collectively outweighing the single record naming Manuela Rojas (1901) by a three-to-one preponderance. The chronological pattern is significant: Juana's marriage in 1901 predates those of Cecilia (1910), Adrian (1913), and Josefa (1922), suggesting Juana is the oldest of Pedro Zuñiga's identified children. The most plausible explanation is that Pedro Zuñiga was first partnered with or married to Manuela Rojas, producing Juana, and that following the dissolution of that union (by death or otherwise), he married or partnered with Polonia Rojas, producing Adrian, Cecilia, and Josefa. Juana Zuñiga is therefore likely a half-sibling of Adrian rather than a full sibling. The conflict does not undermine the identification of Pedro Zuñiga and Polonia Rojas as Adrian's parents.

### Research Scope and Limitations

No baptismal record for Adrian Zuñiga Rojas (born 8 September 1889) was found in any searched collection. FamilySearch 'Bolivia, Catholic Church Records, 1566-2020' (collection 1922463, 9.8 million records) was searched with multiple name variants; FamilySearch 'Bolivia, Marriages, 1630-1940' (collection 1520564) was searched for Pedro Zuñiga's own marriage to Polonia Rojas without result. The FamilySearch AI-transcribed full-text corpus was searched; Bolivia parish records were confirmed absent from that corpus. All four key records were accessed via derivative indexed extraction rather than original image examination — the original parish images are inaccessible via the MCP transport at 1.0–1.7 MB. This derivative-only access is the primary limitation on the confidence tier.

### Conclusion

The evidence strongly indicates that Adrian Zuñiga Rojas (born 8 September 1889, Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia) is the son of **Pedro Zuñiga** and **Polonia Rojas**, both of Arani, Cochabamba, Bolivia. Three independent sources from three different informants — Adrian himself (1913), his sister Cecilia (1910), and his sister Josefa (1922) — all consistently name this couple as the parental unit at San Bartolomé de Arani, corroborated structurally by Adrian's compound surname "Zuñiga Rojas." The one conflicting record (naming Manuela Rojas in 1901) has been resolved by preponderance. The conclusion is rendered at the **probable** tier because all four records were accessed derivatively; examination of the original parish images, if they become accessible, could advance this conclusion to proved.
